title: Writing a GMP Audit Report That Drives Real Change
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How to write a GMP audit report that withstands MHRA scrutiny and drives real change: specific findings, risk-based grading, ALCOA+ and closable CAPA.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a pharmaceutical quality auditor's desk in a softly lit GMP facility, viewed at an angle: a tablet displaying an abstract findings table beside a sterile stainless-steel cleanroom door blurred in the background, a pair of glasses and a clipboard resting on a brushed-metal surface. Cool teal and cyan tones wash over deep navy shadows, lifted by a single subtle green accent of an illuminated status light. Soft natural light rakes from the left, shallow depth of field with creamy bokeh, crisp foreground detail, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, premium editorial atmosphere conveying precision and trust.

faqs.q: How should findings in a GMP audit report be classified?
faqs.a: Findings are typically graded as critical, major or minor. A critical finding signals a significant risk of harm to the patient or a data integrity or fraud concern; a major finding is a significant GMP deviation that is not critical; a minor finding warrants attention but carries limited risk. Apply the grading consistently across findings and across audits so that trends reflect real risk rather than inconsistent judgement, in line with ICH Q9 risk principles.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a correction and a corrective action in a CAPA?
faqs.a: A correction is the immediate fix to the specific problem found, such as completing a missing record. A corrective action addresses the underlying system that allowed the problem to occur, so it cannot recur. Within an ICH Q10 quality system both are needed, but reports that conflate the two tend to leave root causes unresolved and findings recurring at later audits.

faqs.q: How do I make sure a GMP audit report stands up to MHRA inspection?
faqs.a: Support every statement with what was directly observed or with a specific document reviewed, and reference batch records, SOP numbers, rooms or equipment precisely. Use factual, neutral language that addresses systems rather than individuals, and keep the executive summary consistent with the detailed findings. A practical test is whether you would be comfortable defending each sentence to an inspector; if not, rewrite it.
